MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Christopher Hanson of Hanson Law Firm, PC telephone number 415-362-9181 for Plaintiff Theresa Murphy.
Megan Kelly of Severson & Werson, APC telephone number 415-398-3344 appeared by telephone for Defendants The Bank of New York Mellon and Specialized Loan Servicing.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling as to the 3rd cause of action and vacated a portion of its tentative ruling as to the 1st and 2nd causes of action. Defendant The Bank Of New York Mellon And Specialized Loan Servicing LLC's Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint as to the first and second causes of action for declaratory relief is sustained with 20-days leave to amend to permit the Plaintiff, if she can do so in good faith, allege claims that are not barred by the Saterbak case, and their demurrer to the third causes of action is overruled. The first cause of action fails because Ms. Murphy lacks standing to allege a preforeclosure claim that the October 10, 2012 assignment is invalid. (Saterbak v. JP Morgan Chase Bank, N.A. (2016) 245 Cal. App. 4th 808). Her argument that Saterbak only applies to post-closing date claims lacks merit. The second cause of action fails because the assignment occurred before the effective date of the HBOR. (Saterbak). The third cause of action adequately alleges that there was a false publication and pecuniary loss and is not barred by any holding in Saterbak. While much of the parties' briefing focused on the effect of January 27, 2012 judgment, the court does not need to interpret that judgment or ascribe a determination date to it for purposes of deciding this demurrer because it is irrelevant to plaintiff's first and second claims and the validity of the third claim as a pleading matter does not depend on any issue relating to that judgment. The January 2012 judgment, whether it is construed to have a determination date of December 29, 2015 or March 17, 2011, did not apply to MERS because MERS was not a party to the case at the time the judgment was entered and is not named in the judgment. Moreover, regardless of its determination date, the judgment does not affect MERS' purported assignment in October 2012 since that assignment occurred after the entry of the judgment. Accordingly, the January 2012 judgment does not resolve the key issue, as framed by the first amended complaint, whether on October 10, 2012 assignment is valid. That issue depends on whether, at the time of the assignment, MERS had any interest in the deed of trust to assign. Plaintiff says it didn't. Defendants say it did. That is what the court perceives that this case is all about. Mr. Hanson is to prepare a form of order.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
